message.content:
Deep technical study, web research required. I run a deep-research AI pipeline that scores ~0.49-0.51 on DeepResearch Bench (RACE). The live leaderboard leaders score ~0.56-0.58. I need to know EXACTLY what the leaders do that we don't. Be concrete and technical, cite sources.

TARGETS (the current DeepResearch-Bench RACE top systems):
- Baidu Qianfan DeepResearch (qianfan_deepresearch_0430, ~0.58)
- ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch (~0.573)
- Zhipu deep research / GLM (zhipu_deep_research, ~0.571)
- NVIDIA AI-Q (Nemotron-based, ~0.56)
- Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance), Kimi Researcher (Moonshot)
- The DeepResearch Bench paper itself (arXiv 2506.11763) and any top-system tech reports/blogs.

FOR EACH system, find and report (mark "unknown" if unpublished, don't guess):
1. Architecture: planner/orchestrator design, single vs multi-agent, how it decomposes a research question.
2. Retrieval: how many searches/sources, iterative vs one-shot, query reformulation, how it decides when to stop searching (breadth & depth).
3. Generator model: which LLM writes the report; is it a frontier proprietary model or open-weight; any RL/fine-tuning for research-report writing.
4. Test-time compute: reflection loops, self-critique, verification, tree/graph search, how much inference it spends per report.
5. Report synthesis: how it turns evidence into a long analytical report (not just concatenated summaries) — cross-source synthesis, argumentation, structure.

THEN the payoff:
A. What do the TOP-3 systems share that mid-pack systems lack? Rank the techniques by how strongly they seem to correlate with a high RACE Overall.
B. RACE rewards Insight (0.32) + Comprehensiveness (0.29) most. Which of the leaders' techniques specifically drive Insight and Comprehensiveness?
C. A prioritized, concrete list: "to close a ~0.08 RACE gap, the highest-leverage things a challenger pipeline should adopt are ___." Be specific and technical.

Return a structured report with sources. Depth over breadth — real technical detail, not marketing.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll research this thoroughly. This is a substantial technical investigation requiring web research across many systems. Let me use the deep-research harness, which is purpose-built for exactly this kind of multi-source, fact-checked investigation.

input.args:
What do the top DeepResearch Bench (RACE) leaderboard systems do technically that mid-pack systems (~0.49-0.51) don't? Investigate each of these systems in depth and report architecture, retrieval strategy, generator model, test-time compute, and report synthesis:

TARGETS: (1) Baidu Qianfan DeepResearch (qianfan_deepresearch_0430, ~0.58), (2) ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch (~0.573), (3) Zhipu deep research / GLM (zhipu_deep_research, ~0.571), (4) NVIDIA AI-Q (Nemotron-based, ~0.56), (5) Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance), (6) Kimi Researcher (Moonshot), (7) the DeepResearch Bench paper arXiv 2506.11763.

FOR EACH: (1) Architecture — planner/orchestrator, single vs multi-agent, question decomposition. (2) Retrieval — how many searches/sources, iterative vs one-shot, query reformulation, stopping criteria for breadth & depth. (3) Generator model — which LLM writes the report, proprietary vs open-weight, RL/fine-tuning for report writing. (4) Test-time compute — reflection loops, self-critique, verification, tree/graph search, inference spend per report. (5) Report synthesis — how evidence becomes a long analytical report, cross-source synthesis, argumentation, structure. Mark "unknown" if unpublished; do not guess.

PAYOFF: (A) What do TOP-3 systems share that mid-pack lacks? Rank techniques by correlation with high RACE Overall. (B) RACE weights Insight 0.32 + Comprehensiveness 0.29 most — which leader techniques drive Insight and Comprehensiveness specifically? (C) Prioritized concrete list: to close a ~0.08 RACE gap, the highest-leverage things a challenger pipeline should adopt. Be specific and technical. Depth over breadth, cite sources.

content.text:
Run the "deep-research" workflow.

Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report.

When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Phases:
- Scope: Decompose question (from args) into 5 search angles
- Search: 5 parallel WebSearch agents, one per angle
- Fetch: URL-dedup, fetch top 15 sources, extract falsifiable claims
- Verify: 3-vote adversarial verification per claim (need 2/3 refutes to kill)
- Synthesize: Merge semantic dupes, rank by confidence, cite sources

Invoke: Workflow({ name: "deep-research", args: "What do the top DeepResearch Bench (RACE) leaderboard systems do technically that mid-pack systems (~0.49-0.51) don't? Investigate each of these systems in depth and report architecture, retrieval strategy, generator model, test-time compute, and report synthesis:\n\nTARGETS: (1) Baidu Qianfan DeepResearch (qianfan_deepresearch_0430, ~0.58), (2) ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch (~0.573), (3) Zhipu deep research / GLM (zhipu_deep_research, ~0.571), (4) NVIDIA AI-Q (Nemotron-based, ~0.56), (5) Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance), (6) Kimi Researcher (Moonshot), (7) the DeepResearch Bench paper arXiv 2506.11763.\n\nFOR EACH: (1) Architecture — planner/orchestrator, single vs multi-agent, question decomposition. (2) Retrieval — how many searches/sources, iterative vs one-shot, query reformulation, stopping criteria for breadth & depth. (3) Generator model — which LLM writes the report, proprietary vs open-weight, RL/fine-tuning for report writing. (4) Test-time compute — reflection loops, self-critique, verification, tree/graph search, inference spend per report. (5) Report synthesis — how evidence becomes a long analytical report, cross-source synthesis, argumentation, structure. Mark \"unknown\" if unpublished; do not guess.\n\nPAYOFF: (A) What do TOP-3 systems share that mid-pack lacks? Rank techniques by correlation with high RACE Overall. (B) RACE weights Insight 0.32 + Comprehensiveness 0.29 most — which leader techniques drive Insight and Comprehensiveness specifically? (C) Prioritized concrete list: to close a ~0.08 RACE gap, the highest-leverage things a challenger pipeline should adopt. Be specific and technical. Depth over breadth, cite sources." })
I'll run the deep-research workflow. Let me start by loading the web tools and launching parallel search agents across the key angles.
Now let me launch parallel search agents across the five research angles. Each agent will search and fetch sources for its angle.

input.prompt:
You are a web research agent. Research the DeepResearch Bench paper and its RACE evaluation framework. Use WebSearch and WebFetch (you must load them via ToolSearch with query "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" first).

Target sources:
- arXiv 2506.11763 (DeepResearch Bench paper) — abstract, PDF, and any HTML version
- The DeepResearch Bench leaderboard (github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench or the HuggingFace space)
- Any blog posts explaining RACE (Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation)

Find and report CONCRETELY, with direct quotes and source URLs:
1. What exactly is RACE? How does it score? What are the four dimensions (Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability) and their exact weights? Confirm the weights (I believe ~0.32 Insight, 0.29 Comprehensiveness, 0.25 Instruction-Following, 0.14 Readability but verify).
2. Is RACE reference-based (compares against a reference report)? Is it comparative? Does it strip bibliography/citations before judging prose? What model is the judge (Gemini?)?
3. The FACT metric — what does it measure (citation accuracy / support)?
4. The current leaderboard: exact scores for top systems as of mid-2026. Report the ranked list with Overall RACE scores. Especially: Baidu Qianfan DeepResearch, ZTE-Nebula, Zhipu/GLM deep research, NVIDIA AI-Q, Doubao, Kimi Researcher, Gemini/OpenAI/Perplexity deep research, and where a ~0.49-0.51 system would rank.
5. Any published analysis of WHAT distinguishes high-scoring from low-scoring reports on this benchmark.

Return a detailed structured summary with exact numbers and source URLs. Mark anything you cannot verify as "unknown". Do not guess.

input.prompt:
You are a web research agent. Research two Chinese deep-research systems that top the DeepResearch Bench RACE leaderboard. Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load them via ToolSearch with query "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" first).

SYSTEM 1: Baidu Qianfan DeepResearch (model id "qianfan_deepresearch_0430", ~0.58 RACE, the #1 system). Search Baidu AI Cloud Qianfan docs, Baidu tech blogs, Chinese sources (translate), arXiv.
SYSTEM 2: Zhipu AI / GLM deep research (zhipu_deep_research, ~0.571). Zhipu released "AutoGLM" / "GLM-4.5" / a deep research agent, and there is an "AutoGLM-Research" or "GLM-Z1" line. Also look for Zhipu papers on agentic research / "AutoGLM" / "GLM-PC" / RL for web agents.

For EACH system find and report CONCRETELY with source URLs:
1. Architecture — is it a planner/orchestrator multi-agent system or single agent? How does it decompose a research question into sub-questions?
2. Retrieval — how many web searches / sources per report? Iterative multi-round search vs one-shot? Query reformulation? How does it decide when to stop searching (depth/breadth stopping criteria)?
3. Generator model — which LLM writes the final report? (ERNIE for Baidu? GLM-4.5/GLM-Z1 for Zhipu?) Is it RL-trained or fine-tuned specifically for long research-report writing?
4. Test-time compute — reflection loops, self-critique, verification passes, tree/graph search, how much inference per report.
5. Report synthesis — how does it turn evidence into a long analytical report with cross-source synthesis and argumentation?

Also search for any RL-for-deep-research training papers from Baidu or Zhipu (e.g. reinforcement learning with search, agentic RL, "search-R1"-style). Report exact model names, training methods, source URLs. Mark unpublished details as "unknown". Do not guess.

input.prompt:
You are a web research agent. Research two deep-research systems on the DeepResearch Bench RACE leaderboard. Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load them via ToolSearch with query "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" first).

SYSTEM 1: NVIDIA AI-Q / AI-Q Research Assistant / AIQ (Nemotron-based, ~0.56 RACE). NVIDIA published an "AI-Q Blueprint" / "AI-Q Research Assistant" and NeMo Agent toolkit. Search NVIDIA developer blogs, docs, GitHub (NVIDIA/aiqtoolkit or NVIDIA-AI-Blueprints). This one is likely OPEN and well-documented — get real detail.
SYSTEM 2: ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch (~0.573, ranks very high). ZTE (Chinese telecom). Search for "ZTE Nebula deep research", "Nebula大模型", any arXiv/tech report. Likely sparse — report what exists and mark rest unknown.

For EACH find and report CONCRETELY with source URLs:
1. Architecture — planner/orchestrator, single vs multi-agent, how it decomposes a research question. For NVIDIA AI-Q specifically: what is the plan→search→reflect→report loop? Does it use a "reflection" agent?
2. Retrieval — number of searches/sources, iterative vs one-shot, query reformulation, stopping criteria for depth and breadth. NVIDIA AI-Q reportedly has configurable reflection/search iterations — get the exact parameters (num_reflections, etc).
3. Generator model — which LLM writes the report. For NVIDIA: which Nemotron / Llama Nemotron model? Any RL fine-tuning?
4. Test-time compute — reflection loops, self-critique, verification, how many iterations, how much inference per report.
5. Report synthesis — how evidence becomes a long analytical report.

For NVIDIA AI-Q, dig into the actual GitHub repo / blueprint config to find the concrete reflection-loop design and default iteration counts. Report exact parameters and source URLs. Mark unknown where unpublished. Do not guess.

input.prompt:
You are a web research agent. Research two deep-research systems: Kimi Researcher (Moonshot AI) and Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance). Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load them via ToolSearch with query "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" first).

SYSTEM 1: Kimi Researcher (Moonshot AI). There is a detailed Moonshot tech blog on "Kimi-Researcher" describing end-to-end agentic RL. Find it (moonshotai.github.io or moonshot blog). This is HIGH DETAIL — get everything.
SYSTEM 2: Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance / Volcano Engine / Seed). Search ByteDance Seed, Volcano Engine docs, any arXiv. Likely sparse.

For EACH find and report CONCRETELY with source URLs and direct quotes/numbers:
1. Architecture — single agent vs multi-agent, planner/orchestrator, how it decomposes the question. Kimi Researcher is reportedly a SINGLE agent trained end-to-end with RL (not a hand-crafted multi-agent workflow) — confirm and explain why they chose that.
2. Retrieval — Kimi Researcher reportedly averages a large number of search queries per task (I recall ~23 reasoning steps and 200+ URLs, or "on average X search queries") — find the EXACT reported numbers. Iterative? How does it decide when to stop?
3. Generator model — which base model (Kimi k1.5 / k2?), trained how (end-to-end agentic RL, reward design)? What reward signal for research quality?
4. Test-time compute — how many tool calls / reasoning steps per task on average, context management (Kimi has long-context handling), test-time scaling behavior.
5. Report synthesis — how evidence becomes a long analytical report.

For Kimi Researcher specifically extract: the RL method (REINFORCE variant? on-policy?), the average number of search calls and reasoning steps per task, the benchmark numbers they report (HLE, xbench, etc.), and any context-management / long-horizon technique. Report exact numbers and quotes with URLs. Mark unknown where unpublished. Do not guess.

input.prompt:
You are a web research agent. Research the GENERAL techniques that distinguish top deep-research agents, and the frontier baselines (OpenAI Deep Research, Google Gemini Deep Research, Perplexity, Anthropic). # Research Report: Kimi Researcher (Moonshot AI) vs. Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance)

## Summary of source availability

- **Kimi-Researcher: HIGH DETAIL confirmed.** There is a substantive Moonshot tech blog at https://moonshotai.github.io/Kimi-Researcher/ with concrete architecture, RL method, per-task statistics, and benchmark numbers. All figures below are quoted from it.
- **Doubao DeepResearch: SPARSE, as expected.** There is **no dedicated technical report** for a "Doubao DeepResearch" system. DeepResearch is a **product feature** of the Doubao app; the underlying capability comes from ByteDance's **Seed** models (Seed 1.5/1.6/2.0/2.1). ByteDance publishes model technical reports (Seed1.5-Thinking, Seed1.5-VL) and separate academic papers (e.g., DeepResearcher, arXiv:2504.03160), but none is an architecture disclosure for the shipping Doubao DeepResearch agent. Most concrete details below are unpublished/unknown.

---

## SYSTEM 1 — Kimi-Researcher (Moonshot AI)
Primary source (all quotes): https://moonshotai.github.io/Kimi-Researcher/

### 1. Architecture — single agent, end-to-end RL (CONFIRMED)
Kimi-Researcher is a **single autonomous agent trained end-to-end with RL**, explicitly contrasted against hand-crafted multi-agent workflows.

- *"End-to-end agentic reinforcement learning trains a single model to solve problems holistically"* — versus workflow systems that *"assign roles to specialized agents and coordinate the agents using prompt-based workflows."*
- **Why they chose it (their stated rationale):** workflow/multi-agent systems require *"frequent manual updates as models or environments change, reducing scalability and flexibility,"* whereas end-to-end RL *"naturally handles long, on-policy reasoning and adapts to changing tools and environments"* without *"hand-crafted rules or workflow templates."*
- The model learns from **full research trajectories** that *"may include planning, search, browsing, tool calls, code execution, context updates, and the final response."*

### 2. Retrieval — exact reported numbers (CONFIRMED)
- **Reasoning steps:** *"It performs an average of 23 reasoning steps... per task."*
- **URLs:** *"...and explores over 200 URLs per task."*
- **Search queries:** *"Kimi-Researcher can run 70+ search queries per trajectory, with context windows reaching hundreds of thousands of tokens."*
- **Iterative / stopping mechanism:** the action space includes an explicit terminate action — the model emits either tool calls or *"an indication to terminate the trajectory."* A single rollout can extend *"to over 50 iterations"* under context management.

Note: your recollection of "23 reasoning steps and 200+ URLs" is exact. The "search queries" figure is **70+ per trajectory**, not 200+ (the 200+ figure is URLs).

### 3. Generator model & RL method (CONFIRMED)
- **Base model:** *"Built on an internal version of the Kimi k-series model,"* linked to **arXiv:2501.12599 = Kimi k1.5: Scaling Reinforcement Learning with LLMs** (not k2). It is an internal variant of k1.5, not a public checkpoint.
- **RL algorithm:** *"The model is primarily trained using the REINFORCE algorithm"* — i.e., a REINFORCE variant, not PPO/GRPO.
- **On-policy:** *"It is critical to generate strict on-policy data."* They also *"disable LLM engine mechanisms like toolcall format enforcers"* to keep data strictly on-policy.
- **Reward design (two components + shaping):**
  - **Format Reward** — penalizes invalid tool calls / malformed output.
  - **Correctness Reward** — compares the model's answer against ground truth (outcome-based).
  - **Gamma-decay shaping** to prefer shorter successful paths: *"the reward of step i becomes r×γ^(T - i), where r is the outcome reward, T is the number of steps."*
- **Note:** the blog does **not** disclose whether training used an SFT/cold-start phase before RL — unknown.

### 4. Test-time compute & context management (CONFIRMED)
- **Three tools (verbatim):** (1) a **parallel, real-time internal search tool**; (2) a **text-based browser tool** for interactive web tasks; (3) a **coding tool** for automated code execution.
- **Context management:** *"we design a context-management mechanism that allows the model to retain important information while discarding unnecessary documents, thereby extending a single rollout trajectory to over 50 iterations."*
- **Ablation:** *"a model trained with context management uses 30% more iterations"* (vs. without).
- **Long context:** context windows reach *"hundreds of thousands of tokens."*
- **Infra (relevant to test-time/training scaling):** *"Fully asynchronous rollout system"* with Gym-like interfaces; a *"Turn-level Partial Rollout"* mechanism giving *"at least 1.5x"* rollout acceleration; sandbox with MCP stateful sessions.

### 5. Report synthesis (CONFIRMED, qualitative)
The blog frames synthesis via two emergent behaviors rather than a fixed pipeline:
- **Conflicting-information resolution** — iterative hypothesis refinement, cross-referencing multiple source versions before settling on an authoritative answer.
- **Rigorous verification** — even simple queries trigger extra searches and multi-language cross-validation before answering.
Exact report-generation mechanics (templating, section planning) are **not disclosed**.

### 6. Benchmark numbers (CONFIRMED)
- **Humanity's Last Exam (HLE):** **Pass@1 = 26.9%** (state-of-the-art as reported), **Pass@4 = 40.17%**; *"Starting from an initial HLE score of 8.6%, Kimi-Researcher reached 26.9% almost entirely through end-to-end RL training."*
- **xbench-DeepSearch:** **69% pass@1** (averaged over 4 runs), *"outperforming models such as o3 with search tools."*
- **FRAMES, Seal-0, SimpleQA:** reported as strong performance; **no exact numbers** given in the blog.
- Testing dated **June 17–18, 2025**.

---

## SYSTEM 2 — Doubao DeepResearch (ByteDance)
No single authoritative technical source exists. Findings assembled from ByteDance Seed blog, Volcano Engine coverage, and secondary reporting.

### 1. Architecture — UNKNOWN
No public disclosure of single- vs. multi-agent design, planner/orchestrator, or question decomposition for the shipping Doubao DeepResearch feature. Secondary descriptions only say the DeepResearch workflow lets the model *"think, search, and stitch together answers autonomously."* ByteDance's own Seed2.1 blog (https://seed.bytedance.com/en/blog/seed2-1-officially-released-advancing-ai-productivity) describes general agent capabilities (task planning, tool use, multi-step workflows) but contains **no** dedicated research-agent architecture.

### 2. Retrieval — UNKNOWN (no per-task numbers)
No published counts of search queries, reasoning steps, or URLs per task. General framing: Doubao integrates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly with search to enable *"dynamic, multi-turn interactions involving search and tool invocation during reasoning."* Stopping criteria not disclosed.

### 3. Generator model & training — PARTIAL / UNKNOWN
- The DeepResearch feature is powered by the **Seed model family** (Doubao 1.5 Pro → Seed 2.0/2.1). Seed 2.0 launched Feb 14, 2026 (Pro/Lite/Mini/Code) powering the Doubao app (200M+ users).
- ByteDance publishes RL-training details for reasoning models generally (Seed1.5-Thinking, arXiv:2504.13914), but **no research-specific reward design or RL method** for DeepResearch is published. Unknown.

### 4. Test-time compute — UNKNOWN
No tool-call/reasoning-step counts or context-management technique published for DeepResearch specifically. Doubao 1.5 Pro context window is up to **256K tokens** (general model spec, not a DeepResearch-specific figure). Marketing claim: DeepResearch turns tasks that *"once took analysts days"* into **5-to-30-minute** completions.

### 5. Report synthesis — UNKNOWN
Only generic description (search → extract → generate sub-questions → integrate evidence into a coherent answer). No Doubao-specific synthesis mechanics published.

### 6. Benchmark numbers (model-level, not DeepResearch-specific)
- One secondary comparison cites **Doubao (Deep Search) = 26.0%** vs. OpenAI DeepResearch = 42.9% on an unnamed deep-research benchmark (source: Medium analysis, unverified against a primary ByteDance doc — treat as low-confidence).
- Seed 2.0 model-level: **77.3 BrowseComp**, **55.8 Terminal Bench** (these are Seed-model figures reported in press coverage, not a DeepResearch-agent evaluation).

---

## Head-to-head (confidence-tagged)

| Dimension | Kimi-Researcher | Doubao DeepResearch |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Single agent, end-to-end RL (CONFIRMED) | Unknown (no disclosure) |
| Base model | Internal Kimi **k1.5** (arXiv:2501.12599) | Seed family (Doubao 1.5→2.x) |
| RL method | **REINFORCE**, strict on-policy, γ-decay shaping (CONFIRMED) | Unknown |
| Search queries/task | **70+ per trajectory** (CONFIRMED) | Unknown |
| Reasoning steps/task | **~23** (CONFIRMED) | Unknown |
| URLs/task | **200+** (CONFIRMED) | Unknown |
| Iterations/rollout | **50+**, context mgmt (+30% iters) (CONFIRMED) | Unknown |
| Context window | Hundreds of thousands of tokens (CONFIRMED) | 256K (general Seed spec) |
| HLE Pass@1 | **26.9%** (from 8.6% via RL); Pass@4 **40.17%** | Not reported |
| xbench-DeepSearch | **69% pass@1** | Not reported |

## Key sources
- Kimi-Researcher tech blog (primary): https://moonshotai.github.io/Kimi-Researcher/
- Kimi k1.5 base-model paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.12599
- ByteDance Seed 2.1 blog: https://seed.bytedance.com/en/blog/seed2-1-officially-released-advancing-ai-productivity
- Seed1.5-Thinking (RL reasoning): https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.13914
- Related academic (not the Doubao product): DeepResearcher https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.03160

## Explicit unknowns (not guessed)
- Kimi: whether an SFT/cold-start preceded RL; exact FRAMES/Seal-0/SimpleQA numbers.
- Doubao DeepResearch: architecture (single vs multi-agent), planner/orchestrator, per-task retrieval counts, RL method/reward, test-time compute, synthesis mechanics, and any DeepResearch-specific benchmark from a primary ByteDance source. The one Doubao "Deep Search = 26.0%" figure is from secondary reporting and is **low-confidence**.</result>

# Deep Research Agents: Frontier Baselines and General Techniques

Research findings with exact numbers, method names, and source URLs. Items that are unpublished are marked UNKNOWN.

## 1. OpenAI Deep Research

**Architecture / training (from OpenAI's own announcement):**
- Powered by "a version of the upcoming OpenAI **o3** model that's optimized for web browsing and data analysis."
- "Deep research was trained using **end-to-end reinforcement learning on hard browsing and reasoning tasks** across a range of domains, learning to plan and execute a multi-step trajectory to find the data it needs, **backtracking and reacting to real-time information** where necessary." This is OpenAI's explicit claim of RL on real browsing tasks.
- Can browse user-uploaded files, run a Python tool for plots, embed images, and **cite specific sentences/passages** from sources.

**Sources count / test-time compute:** OpenAI's marketing describes "finding, analyzing, and synthesizing **hundreds of online sources**." It "accomplishes in tens of minutes what would take a human many hours" — i.e., test-time compute scales with task difficulty, but exact token/tool-call budgets are UNKNOWN (unpublished).

**Benchmark numbers:**
- **Humanity's Last Exam: 26.6%** accuracy (new high at launch), per OpenAI.
- **BrowseComp: 51.5%** vs standard LLMs below 10% (cited in the agentic-search survey).
- **DeepResearch Bench RACE Overall: 46.98** — Comprehensiveness 46.87, Insight/Depth 45.25, **Instruction-Following 49.27 (highest of any agent)**, Readability 47.14. FACT: 40.79 effective citations, 77.96% citation accuracy.

Sources: https://openai.com/index/introducing-deep-research/ · https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ · https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959

## 2. Google Gemini Deep Research

**Architecture (planner + async + iterative loop):**
- **Interactive research planning**: the agent "autonomously formulates a multi-step investigation plan for interactive user review and modification." In the Gemini API this is explicit: request a plan, iterate with `previous_interaction_id`, then set `collaborative_planning=False` to approve/execute.
- **Asynchronous task management**: "a novel asynchronous task manager... maintains a shared state between the planner and task models, allowing for graceful error recovery without restarting the entire task." API-level: `background=True` + polling.
- **Iterative reason-search loop**: "At each step, the model grounds itself on all information gathered so far, then **identifies missing information and discrepancies** that it wants to explore" — i.e., a gap-analysis-driven retrieval loop.
- Grounds across Google Search, URL Context, Code Execution, MCP, File Search; accepts images/PDFs/docs.

**Model version:** Reported as based on Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking at introduction; the "reciprocal"/RETRO-style synthesis and the specific 1M-context source-holding mechanism are **not confirmed in Google's published docs (UNKNOWN / black-box)**.

**Benchmark numbers (Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research — the current leader):**
- **RACE Overall: 48.88** (highest) — Comprehensiveness **48.53**, Insight/Depth **48.50**, Instruction-Following 49.18, Readability **49.44** (leads on all except IF).
- **FACT: 111.21 effective citations** (far highest — ~2.7x OpenAI's 40.79), 81.44% accuracy.

Sources: https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ · https://www.philschmid.de/deep-research-update · https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/interactions/deep-research · https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/

## 3. Anthropic Multi-Agent Research System (highly relevant)

From the Anthropic engineering blog "How we built our multi-agent research system":
- **Orchestrator-worker pattern**: a **lead agent** analyzes the query, develops a strategy, records the plan in memory, then **spawns 3-5 subagents in parallel** (not serial). Subagents each get their **own context window** and can call **3+ tools in parallel**.
- **Separate citation pass**: after research, the system exits the loop and passes all findings to a dedicated **CitationAgent** that maps report claims to source locations.
- **Why multi-agent wins**: their internal eval had the multi-agent system (**Claude Opus 4 lead + Claude Sonnet 4 subagents**) outperform single-agent Claude Opus 4 by **90.2%**. Parallelism beats slow sequential search on breadth-heavy tasks (e.g., "identify all board members").
- **Token economics**: **agents ≈ 4x chat tokens; multi-agent ≈ 15x chat tokens.** Performance-variance breakdown: **token usage alone explains ~80% of variance**, number of tool calls ~10%, model choice ~5% (three factors ≈ 95%).
- **Breadth/depth heuristic**: "**start wide, then narrow**" — short broad queries first, then progressively narrow. Economics only justified for high-value research (legal due diligence, competitive intel, biomedical review).

Source: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system

## 4. Academic / general findings — what makes reports score well

**RACE evaluation mechanics (DeepResearch Bench, arXiv:2506.11763):** RACE = Reference-based Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation with dynamic weighting; judge model is Gemini-2.5-Pro. 100 PhD-level tasks, 22 fields.
- Scoring formula: `S_final = S_int(R_tgt) / [S_int(R_tgt) + S_int(R_ref)]` (relative to a reference report, range 0-1); `S_int = Σ_d W_d · S_d`.
- **Weights are NOT fixed defaults — they are inferred per task** (`W_d = (1/T) Σ w_d^(j)`, summing to 1). The one published illustrative case weighted **Insight 0.35, Comprehensiveness 0.30**, remainder to Instruction-Following + Readability. (This matches the pattern that Insight+Comprehensiveness dominate weighting on hard tasks.)
- **FACT framework**: Effective Citations (count of valid support relationships) + Citation Accuracy (precision of attributions).

**Agentic-RL-for-search line (survey arXiv:2506.18959 + primary papers):**
- **Iterative retrieval beats single retrieval**: "a single search step often falls short of capturing comprehensive information." Multi-hop iterative search+reason progressively builds context → drives Comprehensiveness.
- **Reasoning-search co-evolution loop**: retrieval and reasoning treated as interdependent and continuously refined — reasoning refines queries, retrieved info refines reasoning.
- **Test-time scaling**: allocate compute to reasoning depth (reasoning-heavy tasks → Insight) vs search breadth (search-heavy → Comprehensiveness).
- Named methods: **Search-R1** (open RL framework interleaving search+reasoning, veRL-based; Search-R1-32B); **R1-Searcher** (two-stage outcome-supervised RL, 7B); **DeepResearcher** (RL in real web environments); **WebThinker**; **ReSearch** (interpretable reasoning); ZeroSearch, WebAgent-R1.
- Repos: https://github.com/PeterGriffinJin/Search-R1 · https://arxiv.org/html/2503.05592 (R1-Searcher)

**WebThinker (arXiv:2504.21776, NeurIPS 2025) — most relevant to report writing:**
- **Autonomous Think-Search-and-Draft**: interleaves reasoning, retrieval, and **report drafting in real time** (not draft-at-end); plus a Deep Web Explorer for gap-triggered navigation. Trained with iterative online DPO.
- **Ablation is the key finding**: removing the **Autonomous Report Draft** capability caused the **largest performance drop** — continuous drafting, not post-hoc writing, is what drives report quality.
- On scientific report writing (Glaive), WebThinker scored **8.1 avg quality**, beating Gemini 2.0 Deep Research and Grok3 DeeperSearch. WebThinker-32B-RL hit SOTA among 32B models with gains up to **+21.5%**.

Sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763 · https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/race-reference-based-adaptive-criteria-evaluation · https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959 · https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.21776

## 5. Techniques repeatedly cited as driving INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS

Consolidated across the sources, the recurring levers (with what each drives):

| Technique | Primarily drives | Evidence source |
|---|---|---|
| **Iterative / multi-hop retrieval** (vs single-shot) | Comprehensiveness | survey 2506.18959 |
| **Reasoning↔search co-evolution loop** (refine queries from intermediate deductions) | Insight + Comprehensiveness | survey 2506.18959 |
| **Gap-analysis / reflection loops** ("identify missing info & discrepancies") | Comprehensiveness | Gemini design; WebThinker Deep Web Explorer |
| **Planning / plan-first (approved outline)** | Instruction-Following + structure | Gemini planner; Anthropic lead-agent plan |
| **"Start wide then narrow" breadth control** | Comprehensiveness | Anthropic |
| **Parallel subagents / more sources** | Comprehensiveness | Anthropic (90.2% gain); Gemini 111 citations |
| **Test-time compute / token budget** | Both (≈80% of variance) | Anthropic |
| **Interleaved continuous drafting** (not draft-at-end) | Report quality overall | WebThinker ablation (largest drop) |
| **RL on real browsing tasks** (end-to-end) | Both | OpenAI; DeepResearcher; Search-R1 |
| **Larger / stronger generator model** | ~5% of variance (smaller lever) | Anthropic |
| **Cross-source contradiction resolution / synthesis (vs summarization)** | Insight | survey; Gemini "discrepancies" grounding |

**Bottom-line pattern relevant to RACE:** Insight and Comprehensiveness carry the highest RACE weights on hard tasks (illustrative 0.35 / 0.30), and the techniques that move them are iterative reason-search loops, gap-driven reflection, breadth-then-depth source expansion, and continuous drafting — while raw generator-model upgrade is a comparatively small lever (~5% of variance in Anthropic's decomposition, dominated by token budget at ~80%).

**Key source URLs:**
- OpenAI: https://openai.com/index/introducing-deep-research/
- Anthropic multi-agent: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/built-multi-agent-research-system
- Gemini: https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/ · https://www.philschmid.de/deep-research-update
- DeepResearch Bench (leaderboard + paper): https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/ · https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763
- Agentic-search survey: https://arxiv.org/html/2506.18959
- WebThinker: https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.21776 · Search-R1: https://github.com/PeterGriffinJin/Search-R1 · R1-Searcher: https://arxiv.org/html/2503.05592

**Marked UNKNOWN (unpublished):** OpenAI DR exact source count / token budget; Gemini DR's exact underlying model version for the current 2.5-Pro DR, its 1M-context source-holding mechanism, and any RETRO/reciprocal synthesis internals (Google has not published these); fixed RACE dimension weights (there are none — they are inferred per task).</result>
